Alright, let's talk about the second A in those three As, appealing. So why are we motivated? Why do we actually want to do habits? What can we do to make sure that we're interested, engaged, and motivated to actually perform the behaviors that we say are important to us? So often, you know, there are things that we say we're interested in that we want to do like cooking healthy meals for ourselves, but in the moment, eating a donut sounds more appealing or more motivating. So how can we shift that and make sure that our habits are actually in this zone of optimal motivation? In order to answer that question, I want to tell you a brief story. So let's imagine that you're playing tennis, right? And you could be playing a tennis match against someone really amazing like Roger Federer or Serena Williams, which while that might be interesting to play a professional tennis player, it probably would get boring or, you know, depressing fairly quickly because you'll be losing all of the points. So might be interesting for a point or two, but pretty soon after that, you start to get bored. On the flip side, you could play tennis against a four-year-old. And again, might be cute or interesting for a point or two, but pretty soon it'll be boring again because you're winning all the points now. But there is a third option. And that third option is that you play someone who's right about at your current ability. They win about half the points, you win about half the points. And what scientists and researchers have found is that challenges like these are actually incredibly motivating because they're right on the cusp of our ability. They're stretching us just a little bit, but it's still achievable. And it's in moments like these when we're operating on the perimeter of our abilities that we're fully engaged, that we're kind of in a state of flow, that we're fully invested in the task at hand. That is when peak motivation occurs. So I like to refer to this as the Goldilocks rule. And the Goldilocks rule states that peak motivation happens when we're operating on the perimeter of our abilities. Not too hard, not too easy, just right, right? Like Goldilocks. So how can we apply this idea? How can we actually figure out if we're operating at a perimeter of our abilities? What does that mean for something to be just too hard, just too easy, not too much of either? Well, scientists offer three different ways of thinking about this problem. So the first is the idea of 50-50 odds. I mentioned in the tennis example that you win about half the points, your opponent wins about half the points. Generally speaking, if you can win right about half the time, then that tends to be incredibly motivating because you know that if you just try, you can capture some of the rewards. So that's the first way of thinking about it. The second way of thinking about it is this idea of about 20-25% more. And that number comes from a couple of studies on finance which show that if you're earning $40,000 a year, you're incredibly motivated by the idea of earning $50,000 a year. Seems like just enough to get you to the next level, but it also still seems achievable. Conversely, if you're earning $100,000 a year, then earning $125,000 a year sounds really motivating. So again, it's like 20-25% increase. Whether you're at $40,000 or $80,000 or $100,000 or $300,000, earning 20-25% more sounds interesting to all of those people because it's just enough to be motivating, but also just enough to be achievable. It's not too hard that you can't get to it. So this is the second way of thinking about being on the perimeter of your abilities, doing 20-25% more than what you're doing now. The third way of thinking about this is through a rule called the 80% rule. So the 80% rule is saying that as long as you're putting in about 80% effort, the mat is about the maximum amount that you want to remain motivated over the long run. It's not too easy. You can't just mail it in and do something at 10% or even 50% of your ability. So you're not dogging it, but you are actually putting in a sustained amount of effort that isn't too hard either. You're not operating at the brink of your ability. If you consider 100% effort to be hair on fire, buildings burning down, you have to pick up a car with your bare hands, that's like the extreme amount of effort that you could give. 80% effort is taken a little bit off of that, but still operating close to your maximum. So this is the third way of thinking about it. Now, obviously each one of these is just a lens for kind of getting you closer to your current ability, a perspective to view the challenges and habits that you're facing through. It's not like motivation doesn't happen at 79% effort and then magically does happen at 80%, right? All of these things are a spectrum. But by using those three different perspectives, we can start to get closer to what actually works for building habits that stick and what keeps you motivated and what maybe is too hard or too easy to sustain your motivation over the long run.
